author: @tomhendry465
text:
Hi Robert .. here's an idea .  Big concave mirror.... water is injected into a flash boiler at the focal point....steam moves down pipe to base of mirror driving a tesla turbine... runs a generator ... generator drives pump and sun tracker and gives handy electric power. Bob's your Uncle.  Good for any where there is sunshine also very efficient.. 1 square meter would give about 900watts.. better than a solar cell .. cheap too .   Probably done before but this could be portable 😮.
Cheers

author: @VacuumTube88

The more transformations a type of energy has to go through in order to be used the lower will be the efficiency and higher will be the cost, there is no escape from that. 
At the moment the best are the thermo solar plants 8mirror and concetrators) and the gravity ones (mine shaft weight drop) that fit in that category but they have other problems about logistics and locations.
For home based production, the best is still photovoltaic connected to a battery.

author: @RyanRoss-d6z
text:
Hey Rob there's another one:

Thermo photo voltaics

You use solar thermal (or any thermal, perhaps even wind to resistive heaters), heat a block of carbon to ~2000C, open a small hole in the containment vessel (ala black body radiation emission cube), place the TVP cell at the exit, place a mirror behind the TVP to reflect the low energy photons back into the box to be reabsorbed.

Presto

author: @junkerzn7312
text:
Its fairly difficult to beat batteries for storage.  95% round-trip efficiency, and roughly 95% transport efficiency (aka the grid), as well as the ability to transport the electricity long distances with low losses.  They are effectively solid state (though the chemical transitions do cause physical stress), and the energy can be stored for a long period of time with very low losses (raw LFP battery cells lose roughly 3-4% per year from self-discharge, for example).

And very low entropy.  Batteries store energy in a form that can easily be converted to virtually any useful form of work

The only down-side is the cost.  But as we have seen, the scale of industry can radically reduce costs which is one reason why battery storage is still the go-to method for grid-level storage.

--

On the other side of the coin we have heat storage in its myriad forms.  Primarily, though, simply heat mass in one form or another.

But as a high-entropy storage medium, heat can only really be converted to ... heat.  It can be used directly for applications requiring heat, such as heating water or heating a home, and in certain cases for industrial uses, but that's about it.  The flexibility ends there.  Converting any high-entropy medium back to low-entropy always has incredible losses associated with it.  The efficiency is very poor.  Always.

A great example of this are steam turbines.  Taking a specific case, molten salt storage for load-shifting solar energy (solar thermal plants) into the evening.   These projects were originally expected to be heads and tails better than simply having fields full of solar panels, but look what happened!   Fields full of solar panels basically won in the end because conversion losses through the turbine are just too high (akin to fossil fuel generation in terms of efficiency), and solar panels are close to zero maintenance while turbines and heat cycles are actually fairly high maintenance.

--

Mechanical storage is basically mechanical or gravity-based potential energy storage.  It is not entropic in of itself (all entropy can be described with mechanical potentials, pretty much).  As a storage medium the things we've come up with have always had a huge problem converting into the storage medium and then getting something useful back out of it.   Both sides tend to have fairly low efficiencies, unfortunately.

--

To me it is clear what is going to win.  It will be batteries for the most part, and direct heat storage will also be economical in certain limited situations (since humans always need water and space heating, at least, and many industrial processes also need direct heat).

Why batteries though?  They seem expensive.  For the same reason why solar panels beat out solar thermal.  At industrial scales the costs either have or will soon beat everything else.  In otherwords, they are getting less expensive every day and other storage methodologies *already* have a hard time competing with utility-scale battery storage.

I can also illustrate this point by using a simple example... the lowly LiFePO4 power station.  It comes in many capacities and capabilities.  It can take energy in, store it, and dole it out for ages to achieve a useful result.   Try doing the same thing with any other type of energy storage medium, even heat storage... and good luck.

Small or big, the lowly LiFePO4 power station more or less demonstrates why batteries will win.

-Matt

replies.author: @ياقوت-ق
replies.text: solar panel is very inefficient ( 18 % - 27 % ) in general, the turbine has high efficiency ( 60 % - 90 % ) with very low maintenance. electrochemical battery is a good technology but has short life. solar thermal can harvest four times more energy from the sun than solar photovoltaic panel per square meter ( according to one commentator ). solar thermal has long life cycle. The reason for the spread of photovoltaic solar panels is the result of consumer companies’ approval of their spread, as they consume silicon, are short-lived, and have low efficiency, in addition to their complexities. All of this will ensure the achievement of profits and maintain the need for them.

replies.author: @junkerzn7312
replies.text:
@ياقوت-ق Urm.   This sounds like AI generated garbage to me.  Nobody cares that a solar panel only collects 1/4th of the sun's power, and you can't compare that to natural gas or any other fueled generation because solar panels do not require any fuel outside regular sunshine.   You aren't paying for your sunshine.

Solar thermal has its uses, but it can only heat things up (like water).  A regular solar panel lasts 40 years with zero maintenance and generates extremely low-entropy, extremely useful electricity.

You would be lucky to get through even 10 years with a solar thermal system without something breaking.  That's the problem when one has pumps and liquids and pipes and heat all in one place.

Company profits?  How absurd.  But go ahead, you can go with solar thermal.  I'll stick with solar panels.

-Matt

With about 178.5° crank duration , with the exhaust ports opening just before the pistons is down having about 181.5° duration, so a valve is open when ever the crank is turning to allow free rotation.  Getting a double acting cylinder to operating similar can be difficult. If poppet valves are used, they will be smaller. To make room for the 1"-1.5" ;shaft and seals, a slide valve or just go single action, and just go double acting on the 6" bore, using two,2.20" valves  opening to .950"using a roller cam 2: 1 rocker, so the cam only requires half the .950" ,( .475" ) on the lobe. It would require a lathe and a bit of mill work. Boring and honing a 24"-30" cylinder isn't the easiest thing to do.  Thinking about the actual HP output, with a second. Stage using the exhaust from the first stage. It may be possible to make closer to 6-8 HP maybe 10, this would be enough power to generate about 6-7kw I'd need enough air to run atleast a hour, or it's not really useful. A practical runtime would be 4-6 hours spinning at 60-80 rpm producing 200-300 ftlb TQ, st 3-4 HP! This should generate 3500-4200 watts , full throttle at 150-200 rpm maybe 8+ HP, should get 5-6 kw atleast, maybe build a boiler with a actual steam engine to compress air for the system. To make it much more useful. Getting about 600 -800 gallons of 400-500 psi air connected to 2" pipe,  with a pair of 1" regulators, and 1" feeds to a 20 gallon tank feeding the engine with a 2" pipe, throttled with a 2":gate valve. And a governor with 2" ball valve , to slow the flow,using a mechanical governor. Maybe use a stepper motor on the gate valve. With a microcontroller reading the pressure , the RPM, and the power required. Use this to set a speed to be most efficient having enough stored air to generate enough power for a day by to 1.3 days or more is ideal, it would last longer than any battery, spinning at 60-200 to rpm, if it's lubed it should last 100 years before it needs a overhauled, even ran daily, for 4 hours. The flywheel  being a 48" magnetic rotor, with 6,8,12mm x 100,120 Maybe 200mm long magnets around the perimeter, with serpentine coils  next to it made from about 16-24 strands of 20-22 gauge wire with the magnets being about 18 mm on center spaced, it's going to be a fairly high frequency AC current, especially at 200 rpm and especially if the generator is on a overdriven shaft. author: @bartronicsecurity
text: For a given square meter of area the most efficient collecter of sun's energy is still the tubes. Over 80 % of the sun's energy is converted to heat in that square meter which is around 4 times the energy collected by solar pv cells per square meter. So to my mind that is the direction we should be looking to collect the sun's energy. It also happens to be cheaper than solar pv per square meter. So converting that heat into electrical energy with some relatively high efficiency electŕo mechanical means is to me the ideal way to go. Question is what is the best way to go after collecting all that high efficiencies heat.

author: @walterbaltzley4546
text: I think the CONCEPT is more important than the implementation.  If we could use the heat from the sun, which we can capture with more than 99% efficiency and use it in a reversible liquid-to-liquid chemical reaction.  And if we can then extract that energy with greater than 40% efficiency - then we have the making of a flow-battery that can compete with molten salts and other solar-thermal methods.

author: @walterbaltzley4546
text: Energy Storage on a grid scale ultimately comes down to $/Watt-Hour - This includes cost of construction and maintenance over time.  There is no singular "best" way to go about it.  Areas close to the ocean can use a form of pumped hydro; Desert areas have lots of sand and might opt for thermal storage.  It all comes down to what resources happen to be most abundant and accessible in a given region.
